description: Property dispute — landlord unlawful eviction, Lahore
document_excerpt:
IN THE COURT OF CIVIL JUDGE, LAHORE
Case No. CV-2024-1142

Petitioner: Mr. Tariq Mahmood, Son of Abdul Mahmood, Resident of House No. 14, Street 7, Model Town, Lahore.
Respondent: Mrs. Saima Akhtar, Owner of the said premises, Resident of 22-B, Gulberg III, Lahore.

FACTS OF THE CASE:
The Petitioner entered into a rental agreement with the Respondent on 1st March 2022 for residential premises located at House No. 14, Street 7, Model Town, Lahore, at a monthly rent of PKR 45,000. The agreement was for a period of two years ending 28th February 2024.

On 15th January 2024, six weeks before the lawful expiry of the tenancy, the Respondent forcibly entered the premises with hired individuals and removed the Petitioner's belongings without a court order. The Petitioner suffered damage to household goods valued at PKR 380,000 and was rendered homeless with his family of four.

The Petitioner contends that the Respondent violated Section 6 of the Punjab Rented Premises Act 2009, which prohibits unlawful eviction without due process. The Petitioner seeks reinstatement of possession, compensation of PKR 500,000 for damages, and costs of litigation.

The Respondent denies unlawful conduct and claims rental arrears of three months totalling PKR 135,000 were outstanding at the time of the eviction.

description: Contract breach — construction project delay, Karachi
document_excerpt:
IN THE HONOURABLE HIGH COURT OF SINDH AT KARACHI
Suit No. HC-2024-0887

Plaintiff: Crescent Builders (Pvt.) Ltd., registered under Companies Act 2017, principal office at Plot 33, SITE Area, Karachi. Represented by CEO Mr. Asad Raza.
Defendant: Coastal Development Authority, a statutory body, Head Office: 5th Floor, Maritime Building, M.T. Khan Road, Karachi.

STATEMENT OF CLAIM:
The Plaintiff was awarded a construction contract on 10th June 2022 for the development of 120 residential units in Block 4, Scheme 45, Karachi, at a total contract value of PKR 2.4 billion, with a completion deadline of 31st December 2023.

The Defendant failed to provide approved building plans and site possession until 1st November 2022, a delay of five months attributable solely to bureaucratic processing failures within the Defendant's engineering department. As a direct result, the Plaintiff incurred additional costs of PKR 87 million for idle machinery, workforce retention, and escalated material prices.

Despite written notices dated 15th January 2023, 3rd April 2023, and 18th September 2023 demanding compensation for delay-related losses and an extension of the completion deadline, the Defendant refused to acknowledge liability.

The Plaintiff claims PKR 87 million in direct losses, PKR 12 million in consequential losses, a 180-day extension of the project timeline, and legal costs.

description: Wrongful termination — senior engineer dismissed without cause, Islamabad
document_excerpt:
IN THE NATIONAL INDUSTRIAL RELATIONS COMMISSION
Case No. NIRC-2024-0334

Complainant: Mr. Bilal Hussain, Senior Electrical Engineer, CNIC 61101-7823456-9, formerly employed at TechPower Solutions (Pvt.) Ltd., Islamabad.
Respondent: TechPower Solutions (Pvt.) Ltd., Plot 12, Sector I-9, Islamabad Industrial Estate.

GROUNDS OF COMPLAINT:
The Complainant was employed by the Respondent since 14th August 2018 under a permanent employment contract at a monthly gross salary of PKR 320,000. He served as Senior Electrical Engineer and received consistently positive performance appraisals for five consecutive years, the most recent rating being 'Exceeds Expectations' dated 30th March 2024.

On 12th June 2024, without any prior warning, show-cause notice, or disciplinary hearing, the Respondent issued a termination letter citing 'restructuring' as justification. No severance beyond the statutory one-month notice was offered.

The Complainant contends this constitutes wrongful termination in violation of the Industrial and Commercial Employment (Standing Orders) Ordinance 1968. The Complainant was not offered retrenchment compensation as required under Section 12 of the Ordinance. The true reason for termination, the Complainant alleges, was his formal complaint to the Federal Ombudsman regarding unpaid overtime of PKR 480,000 filed on 1st May 2024.

Relief sought: reinstatement with full back pay, PKR 480,000 in unpaid overtime, and retrenchment compensation of PKR 960,000.

description: Road traffic accident — negligence claim against reckless driver
document_excerpt:
IN THE COURT OF THE MOTOR ACCIDENT CLAIMS TRIBUNAL, RAWALPINDI
Claim No. MACT-2024-0219

Claimant: Mrs. Nazia Shaheen, Wife of Late Mr. Shahid Khan, Resident of House 7, Satellite Town, Rawalpindi.
Respondent No. 1: Mr. Farhan Iqbal, Driver, Resident of Dhoke Ratta, Rawalpindi.
Respondent No. 2: National Insurance Corporation, Ground Floor, Bank Square, Rawalpindi.

STATEMENT OF FACTS:
On 22nd March 2024 at approximately 8:45 AM, the deceased Mr. Shahid Khan, aged 42, was crossing Murree Road at the designated pedestrian crossing near Committee Chowk when he was struck by a Toyota Hiace van (Registration No. RWP-2019-4477) driven by Respondent No. 1 at excessive speed.

Eyewitness accounts and CCTV footage from a nearby bank confirm that Respondent No. 1 ran a red signal, was travelling at an estimated 85 km/h in a 50 km/h zone, and made no attempt to brake before impact. Mr. Shahid Khan was a government employee drawing a monthly salary of PKR 95,000 and was the sole breadwinner for a family of six.

The Claimant seeks compensation of PKR 12,000,000 calculated on the basis of lost future earnings, PKR 500,000 for funeral expenses and bereavement, and PKR 200,000 for medical expenses incurred before death.

description: Banking fraud — unauthorised wire transfers from business account
document_excerpt:
IN THE BANKING COURT NO. 2, KARACHI
Case No. BC-2024-0561

Plaintiff: Horizon Textile Mills (Pvt.) Ltd., registered at SECP, principal office at 45-A, Korangi Industrial Area, Karachi.
Defendant No. 1: United Commercial Bank Ltd., Main Branch, I.I. Chundrigar Road, Karachi.
Defendant No. 2: Mr. Imran Saleem, Branch Manager, United Commercial Bank Ltd.

SUBJECT MATTER:
The Plaintiff maintains a corporate current account (Account No. 01234-567890-03) with Defendant No. 1. Between 3rd July 2024 and 19th August 2024, fourteen unauthorized wire transfers totalling PKR 34,750,000 were executed from the Plaintiff's account to three external accounts, none of which are known to or authorised by the Plaintiff's board of directors.

The Plaintiff contends that Defendant No. 2, the Branch Manager, processed these transfers without the dual-signatory authorisation mandated by the Plaintiff's board resolution and the account operating instructions on file. Internal bank logs obtained via court order confirm that Defendant No. 2 overrode the dual-signatory system on each occasion using supervisor-level credentials.

Despite a formal complaint lodged on 22nd August 2024 and a freeze order obtained from this court on 25th August 2024, only PKR 8,200,000 of the misappropriated funds have been recovered.

The Plaintiff claims recovery of PKR 34,750,000, interest at 18% per annum from the date of each transfer, and punitive damages of PKR 5,000,000.

description: Child custody dispute following divorce, Lahore Family Court
document_excerpt:
IN THE FAMILY COURT, LAHORE
Case No. FC-2024-2287

Applicant: Mr. Kamran Ali, Son of Muhammad Ali, Resident of 33-C, DHA Phase 5, Lahore.
Respondent: Mrs. Sana Kamran (formerly Sana Fatima), Resident of 8, Askari 10, Lahore.

APPLICATION FOR CUSTODY:
The parties were married on 12th April 2015 and have two minor children: Ayaan Ali (born 3rd September 2017, aged 6) and Maryam Ali (born 19th January 2020, aged 4). The parties underwent dissolution of marriage by the Family Court vide decree dated 30th June 2024.

The Applicant seeks primary custody of both children on the grounds that the Respondent has relocated the children to her parents' home in Faisalabad without court permission, disrupting the children's schooling at Beaconhouse School, DHA Campus, and denying the Applicant visitation rights since 15th July 2024.

The Applicant further submits that as the children's father and primary financial provider, contributing PKR 180,000 monthly towards children's expenses, he is best positioned to provide educational and social continuity. A welfare report commissioned by the court dated 5th October 2024 notes that both children have expressed comfort with both parents.

The Respondent contends that the move to Faisalabad was necessitated by the Applicant's alleged domestic violence, citing a police report dated 2nd April 2024.

description: Armed robbery criminal case — FIR and charge sheet
document_excerpt:
IN THE COURT OF SESSIONS JUDGE, FAISALABAD
Session Case No. SC-2024-0778

State vs. Accused: (1) Zafar Iqbal, Son of Iqbal Hussain, CNIC 33202-4567891-3, Resident of Mohallah Gulshan, Faisalabad; (2) Rashid Mehmood, Son of Mehmood Khan, CNIC 33202-9876543-7, Resident of Chak No. 45, Faisalabad.

CHARGES:
The accused persons are jointly charged under Section 392 (robbery) and Section 34 (common intention) of the Pakistan Penal Code 1860.

PROSECUTION CASE:
On the night of 18th September 2024 at approximately 11:20 PM, the accused persons, armed with a pistol and a knife respectively, intercepted Mr. Aslam Pervez outside his jewellery shop at Main Bazaar, Faisalabad, upon his closing of the business premises. The accused threatened the victim at gunpoint and robbed him of cash amounting to PKR 750,000, gold jewellery valued at PKR 1,200,000, and a mobile phone.

The accused were apprehended by Faisalabad Police within 72 hours based on CCTV footage and witness identification. Recovered from their possession: PKR 340,000 in cash, jewellery identified by the victim as matching stolen items, and the pistol used, which was unlicensed.

The prosecution presents: FIR No. 887/2024 registered at City Police Station; CCTV footage authenticated by forensic laboratory; three eyewitness testimonies; and laboratory report confirming fingerprint match of Accused No. 1 on the shop shutter.

description: Medical negligence — surgical error causing permanent disability
document_excerpt:
IN THE HIGH COURT OF BALOCHISTAN AT QUETTA
Writ Petition No. WP-2024-0445

Petitioner: Mr. Abdul Rehman Baloch, Son of Ghulam Hussain, CNIC 54400-3214567-1, Resident of Sariab Road, Quetta.
Respondent No. 1: Provincial Government of Balochistan, through Secretary Health.
Respondent No. 2: Dr. Pervez Mengal, Orthopaedic Surgeon, Civil Hospital Quetta.

FACTS:
The Petitioner was admitted to Civil Hospital Quetta on 5th August 2024 for a routine appendectomy. During the surgical procedure conducted by Respondent No. 2, a surgical swab was negligently left in the Petitioner's abdominal cavity. The Petitioner was discharged on 8th August 2024 without complication being detected.

The Petitioner presented with severe abdominal pain, fever, and infection on 22nd August 2024. Emergency re-surgery at Quetta Institute of Medical Sciences on 25th August 2024 confirmed and removed the retained swab, but the resulting peritonitis caused permanent damage to the Petitioner's large intestine, requiring a colostomy.

An independent medical review by a panel of three specialists, obtained privately on 15th September 2024, concluded that the retained swab represented a clear failure of standard surgical count procedures. The Petitioner, aged 34, was a daily-wage construction worker earning PKR 1,200 per day and is now permanently disabled.

Relief sought: PKR 15,000,000 in compensation, PKR 2,300,000 in medical expenses to date, and directions for departmental inquiry against Respondent No. 2.

description: Trademark infringement — counterfeit garments sold under registered brand
document_excerpt:
IN THE INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY TRIBUNAL, LAHORE
Case No. IPT-2024-0193

Plaintiff: Khaadi Enterprises (Pvt.) Ltd., a company incorporated under Companies Act 2017, Trademark Registration No. TM-2009-00441, principal office at 14-C, Khayaban-e-Ittehad, DHA Phase 6, Karachi.
Defendant: Mr. Saleem Traders, a sole proprietorship operated by Mr. Jawad Saleem, Shop No. 17, Anarkali Bazaar, Lahore.

GROUNDS OF ACTION:
The Plaintiff is the registered trademark owner of the 'KHAADI' word mark and logo (Registration No. TM-2009-00441), protected under the Trade Marks Ordinance 2001 across Classes 24 and 25 covering textile and garment products.

In September 2024, the Plaintiff's enforcement team, acting on market intelligence, conducted a test purchase at the Defendant's premises and acquired 12 garments bearing marks identical to the Plaintiff's registered trademarks. Laboratory analysis conducted by SGS Pakistan confirmed that these were counterfeit products manufactured outside the Plaintiff's supply chain.

A raid conducted by the Federal Investigation Agency on 10th October 2024, accompanied by the Plaintiff's representatives, resulted in seizure of 4,300 counterfeit garment pieces with an estimated retail value of PKR 6,450,000 and manufacturing moulds bearing the Plaintiff's copyrighted designs.

The Plaintiff seeks a permanent injunction, delivery up of all infringing goods, PKR 10,000,000 in damages, and an account of profits made by the Defendant through sale of counterfeit goods.

description: Environmental damage — factory effluent polluting agricultural land
document_excerpt:
IN THE ENVIRONMENTAL PROTECTION TRIBUNAL, PUNJAB
Case No. EPT-2024-0082

Complainants: Residents of Village Kot Lakhpat (represented by Ch. Manzoor Ahmad, Chairman Union Council), District Sheikhupura.
Respondent: Punjab Chemicals Industries Ltd., Factory Registration No. PEPA-2015-0334, Kot Lakhpat Industrial Zone, Sheikhupura.

COMPLAINT:
The Complainants represent 340 farming families whose agricultural land, totalling 1,200 acres, borders the Respondent's chemical manufacturing facility. Since approximately April 2023, the Respondent has been discharging untreated industrial effluent directly into the irrigation canal serving the Complainants' fields.

Water quality tests conducted by the Punjab Environmental Protection Agency on 12th June 2024 and 8th September 2024 confirmed presence of hexavalent chromium, lead, and arsenic at concentrations 40 to 120 times the permissible limits under the Pakistan Environmental Protection Act 1997 and National Environmental Quality Standards.

As a result of soil and water contamination, the 2024 wheat and rice crops failed entirely on approximately 780 of the 1,200 affected acres. Twenty-three residents have been diagnosed with heavy metal toxicity by District Headquarter Hospital Sheikhupura. Livestock deaths have been reported on 18 farms.

The Complainants seek: immediate cessation of effluent discharge; remediation of contaminated land and water; compensation of PKR 45,000,000 for crop losses; PKR 8,000,000 for medical expenses; and criminal referral under Section 17 of the Pakistan Environmental Protection Act 1997.
